title: 10 best herbal creams for ulcerative colitis

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Curcumin, a bioactive compound found in turmeric, has potent anti-inflammatory effects, which can reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the gut, alleviating symptoms of UC.
Antioxidant properties: Turmeric's antioxidant capacity helps neutralize free radicals that contribute to tissue damage and colonic inflammation, promoting healing and reducing symptoms of UC.
Gut-protective compounds: Turmeric contains compounds that help maintain the integrity of the gut lining, reducing permeability and preventing further irritation and damage caused by UC.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Curcuma longa, also known as turmeric, creams helps with ulcerative colitis because of its potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The active compound curcumin in turmeric has been shown to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the gut, which are key contributors to the development of ulcerative colitis. By inhibiting pro-inflammatory enzymes and promoting the production of anti-inflammatory cytokines, curcumin creams can help to reduce symptoms such as diarrhea, abdominal pain, and rectal bleeding, improving overall quality of life for those affected by this condition.
remedy_constituents: Curcumin: Curcumin, a polyphenolic compound, has potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the colon, alleviating symptoms of ulcerative colitis.

Demethoxycurcumin (DMC): DMC, another polyphenolic compound, has been shown to inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines, which contribute to the development of ulcerative colitis.

Volatile oil β-sesquiphellandrene: β-sesquiphellandrene, a sesquiterpene, has been found to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help protect the colon from oxidative damage and inflammation associated with ulcerative colitis.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: Rhyzomes are the most used part due to their high concentration of curcumin, a bioactive compound with anti-inflammatory properties that help alleviate symptoms of ulcerative colitis.
Roots: Roots of Curcuma longa are also commonly used for their ability to reduce inflammation and promote healing in the colon, making them a popular ingredient in creams for ulcerative colitis.
Seeds: Seeds of Curcuma longa contain a high amount of curcumin, which is often extracted and used in creams to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress associated with ulcerative colitis.
remedy_recipe: Infuse 2 tablespoons of dried curcuma longa root in 1 cup of carrier oil like coconut oil for 2 hours.
Strain the infused oil through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean glass container.
Combine 1/2 cup of the infused oil with 1/4 cup of beeswax and 1/4 cup of shea butter in a double boiler.
Whip the mixture with an electric mixer until it thickens and becomes a smooth cream consistency after 5 minutes.
Package the cooled curcuma longa cream in airtight containers and store them in the refrigerator for up to 6 months.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Ginger contains anti-inflammatory compounds like gingerols and shogaols, which can help reduce inflammation in the gut lining, alleviating symptoms of ulcerative colitis such as abdominal pain, diarrhea, and rectal bleeding.

Antioxidant properties: Ginger's antioxidant properties can help protect the colon mucosa from oxidative stress and damage caused by free radicals, which can exacerbate symptoms of ulcerative colitis.

Spasmolytic activity: The spasmolytic compounds in ginger, such as gingerol, can help relax the smooth muscle in the gut, reducing spasms and cramping associated with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) and ulcerative colitis.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale, also known as ginger, creams helps with ulcerative colitis because of its anti-inflammatory properties. The active compounds in ginger, such as gingerols and shogaols, have been shown to inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, which contribute to the inflammation and damage associated with ulcerative colitis. Additionally, ginger's ability to relax the smooth muscles in the gastrointestinal tract can help to reduce spasms and improve bowel movements, making it a potential natural remedy for managing ulcerative colitis symptoms.
remedy_constituents: Gingerols: These lipophilic compounds have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the colon, a key factor in ulcerative colitis.
Shogaols: Shogaols have been shown to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities, which can help mitigate the oxidative stress and inflammation associated with ulcerative colitis.
Terpenoids (specifically, zingerone): Zingerone has been demonstrated to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help reduce inflammation and promote healing in the colon.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: They are the most commonly used part of ginger due to their high concentration of active compounds like gingerols and shogaols, which have anti-inflammatory properties.
Roots: The roots of ginger contain a significant amount of gingerol, a key compound that helps reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms of ulcerative colitis.

Leaves: The leaves of ginger are also used in creams due to their high content of flavonoids, which have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help soothe ulcerative colitis symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 50g of fresh zingiber officinale roots at dawn when the plant is in full bloom.
Dry the harvested roots in a low-temperature oven at 50°c for 24 hours.
Grind 20g of dried zingiber officinale roots into a fine powder using a spice grinder.
Combine 10g of the powdered zingiber officinale with 50g of shea butter and 20g of coconut oil.
Mix the ingredients in a double boiler at 60°c for 30 minutes until a smooth cream forms.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory activity: Aloe vera's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as aloin and aloe-emodin, help reduce inflammation in the colon, which is a primary symptom of ulcerative colitis.

Wound-healing properties: Aloe vera's gel contains vitamins A, C, and E, as well as other nutrients that promote wound healing by stimulating collagen synthesis, improving tissue repair, and reducing oxidative stress.

Antioxidant activity: The antioxidants present in aloe vera, including vitamin C and beta-carotene, help neutralize free radicals that can damage colon lining cells and contribute to the development of ulcerative colitis.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Aloe vera, also known as aloe, creams helps with ulcerative colitis because it has potent anti-inflammatory properties that soothe and calm the digestive tract. The gel extracted from the aloe plant contains compounds such as aloin and aloe-emodin, which reduce inflammation and promote healing of the damaged gut lining. By reducing inflammation and promoting healing, aloe vera creams can help alleviate symptoms of ulcerative colitis, including abdominal pain, diarrhea, and rectal bleeding, providing relief and comfort to those affected.
remedy_constituents: Aloe-emodin: Aloe-emodin, a anthraquinone compound, has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress associated with ulcerative colitis.
Glycoproteins: Aloe vera's glycoproteins, particularly acemannan, may help modulate the immune system and promote healing in the gut, reducing symptoms of ulcerative colitis.

Anthraquinones (specifically, aloin and aloe-emodin): The anthraquinones present in Aloe vera may help stimulate the movement of the gut and promote the healing of the intestinal lining, reducing symptoms of diarrhea, abdominal pain, and rectal bleeding associated with ulcerative colitis.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They are the most commonly used part due to their high concentration of aloe-emodin, a compound that has anti-inflammatory properties.
Gel from Leaves: A clear gel extracted from the inner part of the leaves, which has soothing and anti-inflammatory effects.
Aloin from Leaves: A compound found in the outer layer of the leaves, which has anti-inflammatory and anti-oxidant properties.
remedy_recipe: Mix 2 tablespoons of aloe vera gel with 2 ounces of distilled water in a bowl.
Combine 2 teaspoons of shea butter with 2 teaspoons of coconut oil in a separate bowl.
Whisk the aloe mixture with a hand mixer for 2 minutes or until smooth and creamy.
Gradually add the shea butter mixture to the aloe mixture while continuously whisking for 5 minutes.
Pour the final mixture into a glass jar and store in the refrigerator for up to 2 weeks.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Slippery elm's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as mucilages, can reduce inflammation in the gut and alleviate symptoms like abdominal pain and cramping.

Antioxidant effects: Slippery elm's antioxidant compounds may help neutralize free radicals that can contribute to inflammation and tissue damage in ulcerative colitis, promoting overall healing and recovery.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Ulmus rubra, also known as slippery elm, creams helps with ulcerative colitis because of its anti-inflammatory and soothing properties. remedy_recipe: Harvest 100g of dried ulmus rubra bark and 50g of dried leaves in the early morning to ensure highest potency.
Infuse the bark in 500ml of organic carrier oil such as sweet almond oil for 2-3 weeks in a cool dark place.
Strain the infused oil through cheesecloth into a clean glass container and discard the solids.
Add 20g of beeswax to the infused oil and heat the mixture in a double boiler until the beeswax is fully incorporated.
Pour the cooled mixture into small tin containers and store in the refrigerator to thicken the cream to desired consistency.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Herbal licorice creams contain anti-inflammatory compounds like glycyrrhizin, which helps to reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms in individuals with ulcerative colitis.

Mucilages and demulcents: The mucilages and demulcents present in herbal licorice creams create a protective barrier on the mucous membranes of the gut, soothing and healing damaged tissues and reducing irritation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra, also known as licorice, creams helps with ulcerative colitis because of its anti-inflammatory properties. The active compound glycyrrhizin reduces inflammation and irritation in the digestive tract, alleviating symptoms such as abdominal pain and diarrhea. Additionally, licorice cream's antimicrobial properties help combat bacterial overgrowth, which can exacerbate the condition. remedy_recipe: Harvest 50-100 grams of dried glycyrrhiza glabra roots and grind them into a fine powder using a spice grinder.
Steep the powdered glycyrrhiza glabra in 500 milliliters of distilled water for 30 minutes to 1 hour to create a liquid extract.
Combine the liquid extract with 100 grams of beeswax and 100 grams of coconut oil in a double boiler to melt the mixture.
Blend the mixture with 50 milliliters of jojoba oil and 20 grams of vitamin e oil to create a smooth cream consistency.
Pour the cream mixture into a sterilized container and let it cool and solidify at room temperature for 2-3 hours.

remedy_recipe: Weigh and combine 50g of dried althaea officinalis roots with 200ml of distilled water in a saucepan.
Boil the mixture over medium heat for 10-15 minutes then reduce the heat to low.
Steep the mixture for 30-40 minutes then strain the liquid through a cheesecloth into a bowl.
Combine 50g of beeswax, 50g of shea butter, and 20g of vitamin e oil in a heat-resistant bowl.
Melt the mixture in a double boiler over low heat then mix in the althaea officinalis liquid and allow to cool.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 50g of dried arctium lappa roots from a trusted supplier and sift them through a fine mesh.
Combine the sifted arctium lappa roots with 20g of beeswax and 20g of coconut oil in a double boiler.
Heat the mixture over low heat for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the beeswax has fully melted.
Remove the double boiler from heat and let the mixture cool and thicken for 1 hour, then whisk in 10g of shea butter.
Pour the cooled mixture into glass jars and store them in a cool dry place for up to 6 months.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh crataegus monogyna leaves and stems in the morning after dew has dried for optimal potency.
Chop the harvested plant material into small pieces and measure out 20 grams of dried material per 100 milliliters of carrier oil.
Combine the chopped plant material with the measured amount of carrier oil in a clean glass jar and store it in a cool dark place for four weeks.
Strain the mixture through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass container discarding the solids after three days.
Transfer the filtered herbal infusion to sterile cream bases at a ratio of one part herbal infusion to two parts cream base and whip until smooth for topical application.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: English lavender creams helps with ulcerative colitis because they contain calming properties that soothe irritated mucous membranes in the colon. remedy_recipe: Harvest and dry 50g of fresh lavender flowers, processing them into a fine powder within 24 hours.

Heat the mixture at 60°c for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the beeswax is fully incorporated and melted.

remedy_properties: Menthol content: Peppermint creams contain menthol, which has been shown to reduce inflammation and relax muscles in the digestive tract, providing relief from symptoms such as abdominal pain and cramping.

Anti-inflammatory effects: The anti-inflammatory compounds present in peppermint oil, such as flavonoids and phenolic acids, help to reduce inflammation in the colon, which can contribute to ulcerative colitis symptoms like diarrhea and rectal bleeding.

Antioxidant properties: Peppermint creams contain antioxidants that help protect the gut lining from oxidative stress and damage caused by free radicals, which can exacerbate ulcerative colitis symptoms and increase disease severity.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in menthol and other bioactive compounds, peppermint roots are known for their anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, making them effective for soothing digestive issues.

Rhyzomes: The underground stems of peppermint plants contain a high concentration of menthone and other volatile oils, which help to calm irritated tissues and reduce inflammation in the digestive tract.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh peppermint leaves from your garden or store-bought organic peppermint leaves in small batches for processing.
Dry the freshly harvested peppermint leaves using a food dehydrator set at 135°f for 2-3 hours or air-drying on paper towels.
Grind the dried peppermint leaves into a fine powder using a spice grinder or mortar and pestle, yielding approximately 1 cup of mentha powder.
Melt 8 tablespoons of beeswax pellets in a double boiler set up over boiling water for 10-15 minutes until fully liquefied.
Combine the melted beeswax with 2 ounces of sweet almond oil, 2 teaspoons of vitamin e oil, and 1 cup of mentha powder, stirring well to combine and pour into sterilized glass jars.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Red clover creams helps with ulcerative colitis because it is a rich source of isoflavones, which have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract. The creams also contain flavonoids, which have antioxidant effects that can help protect the mucous membranes from damage and promote healing. Additionally, red clover has been shown to modulate the immune system, reducing the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and promoting a more balanced response to stressors in the body. This can help alleviate symptoms such as diarrhea, abdominal pain, and bloody stools.
remedy_properties: Isoflavones: Red clover creams contain isoflavones, which have been shown to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the gut, thereby alleviating symptoms of ulcerative colitis.

Phytoestrogens: The phytoestrogens present in red clover creams can help regulate estrogen levels and reduce inflammation in the gut, which may improve symptoms of ulcerative colitis such as diarrhea and abdominal pain.

Antioxidants: Red clover creams are rich in antioxidants, which help protect the gut lining from damage caused by free radicals and oxidative stress, thereby reducing the risk of intestinal inflammation and damage associated with ulcerative colitis.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of red clover contain a high concentration of isoflavones, which have anti-inflammatory properties and can help soothe inflamed colonic tissues.
Seeds: Red clover seeds contain triterpenoid saponins, which have been shown to reduce inflammation and improve symptoms in patients with ulcerative colitis.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality fresh or dried trifolium pratense flowers.
Combine one part of the dried flowers with four parts of carrier oil such as coconut or sweet almond oil.
Steep the mixture for 2-4 weeks in a cool dark place allowing the herbs to infuse into the oil.
Strain the infused oil through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean container discarding the solids.
Whip the filtered oil with 10% beeswax and 1 tablespoon of vitamin e oil until it reaches a smooth creamy consistency.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Sage creams helps with ulcerative colitis because they contain natural anti-inflammatory compounds that soothe and calm irritated digestive tissues. The herb's antioxidants and flavonoids work to reduce inflammation in the colon, promoting a healthy balance of gut bacteria and improving digestion. Additionally, sage has been shown to inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory chemicals, helping to reduce symptoms such as abdominal pain, diarrhea, and bloating associated with ulcerative colitis. By applying topically, these creams can provide targeted relief for affected areas.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Herbal sage creams contain anti-inflammatory compounds such as rosmarinic acid and eucalyptol, which help reduce inflammation in the colon, alleviating symptoms of Ulcerative Colitis.
Antioxidant properties: Sage contains antioxidants that neutralize free radicals and protect the gut lining from oxidative stress, reducing damage to the mucosa and promoting healing.

Gastroprotective compounds: Herbal sage creams contain compounds like flavonoids and terpenes that help prevent gastric ulcers by reducing acid production, increasing mucus production, and improving gut motility.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the sage plant contain bioactive compounds that help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the gut, making them suitable for treating ulcerative colitis.
Barks: The barks of the sage plant have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of ulcerative colitis by reducing inflammation and preventing infection.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality salvia officinalis leaves and chop them into small pieces.
Combine chopped salvia officinalis with 100g of beeswax and 200ml of coconut oil in a double boiler.
Heat the mixture over low heat for 30 minutes stirring occasionally until the beeswax is fully incorporated.
Add 20ml of vitamin e oil and 10g of aloe vera gel to the cooled mixture and stir well.
Pour the final mixture into sterilized containers and let it solidify at room temperature for 60 minutes.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: St John's Wort creams helps with ulcerative colitis because they contain hyperforin, a key compound that has anti-inflammatory properties. When applied topically, this cream can reduce inflammation in the gut lining, alleviating symptoms such as abdominal pain and diarrhea. Additionally, St John's Wort's antimicrobial activity can help eliminate harmful bacteria from the colon, promoting a healthier gut microbiome. By targeting both inflammation and infection, St John's Wort creams provide relief for individuals suffering from ulcerative colitis.
remedy_properties: Hyperforin: This bioactive compound has anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects, which can help reduce inflammation in the gut and alleviate symptoms of Ulcerative Colitis.
Hypotensive effect: The vasodilatory properties of St John's Wort may help improve blood flow to the colon, reducing inflammation and promoting healing of ulcers and damaged tissue.

Antioxidant activity: St John's Wort's antioxidant compounds can protect against oxidative stress and damage caused by free radicals in the gut, which may contribute to Ulcerative Colitis symptoms and progression.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in hyperforin and hypericin, which have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties that help soothe and protect the colon lining.
Leaves: Containing flavonoids and phenolic acids, these leaves have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, reducing inflammation and oxidative stress in the gut.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality st john's wort flowers and leaves from a reputable source.
Dry the plant material using a food dehydrator on low heat for 6 hours.
Mix 250 milliliters of coconut oil with 50 grams of dried hypericum perforatum flowers and leaves in a clean glass jar.
Steep the mixture at room temperature for 24 to 48 hours, shaking the jar daily.
Strain the mixture through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean glass container, discarding the solids.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Yarrow creams helps with ulcerative colitis because it contains flavonoids, which have potent anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract. The cream's active compounds also possess antimicrobial and antioxidant abilities, which can aid in soothing irritated mucous membranes and promoting a healthy gut environment. Additionally, yarrow's natural anti-inflammatory effects may help alleviate symptoms such as abdominal pain, diarrhea, and rectal bleeding associated with ulcerative colitis. remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Yarrow creams contain anti-inflammatory compounds like sesquiterpenes, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, alleviating symptoms of ulcerative colitis.

Antioxidant properties: Yarrow's antioxidant properties help neutralize free radicals that can damage the gut lining and exacerbate symptoms of ulcerative colitis, promoting healing and reducing oxidative stress.

Wound-healing activity: Yarrow's wound-healing properties, attributed to its flavonoids and other bioactive compounds, may help promote mucosal healing and tissue repair in the colon, potentially reducing inflammation and improving symptoms.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of yarrow contain sesquiterpenes and other compounds that have anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in soothing and calming irritated tissues.
Buds: Yarrow buds are rich in flavonoids and polyphenols, which have antioxidant properties that help protect the gut lining from oxidative damage and reduce inflammation.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh achillea millefolium leaves and dry them using air circulation or desiccation for at least two weeks.
Grind the dried achillea millefolium leaves into a fine powder using a spice grinder or mortar and pestle.
Combine 20 grams of ground achillea millefolium with 100 milliliters of cold-pressed sweet almond oil in a double boiler.
Heat the mixture over low heat for 30 minutes, then cool it to room temperature before straining out any solids through cheesecloth.
Transfer the infused oil to glass jars and store them in the refrigerator for at least two weeks or until the cream is ready to use.

intro_desc: Herbal creams for ulcerative colitis are topical preparations derived from plants that help alleviate symptoms of this inflammatory bowel disease by soothing the digestive tract and reducing inflammation. The best herbal creams for ulcerative colitis include Aloe barbadensis, Curcuma longa, Glycyrrhiza glabra, Calendula officinalis, Aloe vera, Zingiber officinale, Ulmus rubra, Symphytum officinale, Taraxacum officinale, and Althaea officinalis. If left untreated, ulcerative colitis can cause severe diarrhea, abdominal pain, and weight loss, negatively impacting daily life, social relationships, and overall well-being.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to ulcerative colitis that are treated with herbal creams are inflammatory bowel diseases such as Crohn's disease, irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), and proctalgia. Herbal creams containing ingredients like aloe vera, chamomile, and calendula have anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe and calm irritated mucous membranes in the digestive tract, reducing symptoms of abdominal pain, diarrhea, and constipation. These herbal remedies may also promote healing and reduce inflammation in the affected areas.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal creams that help with ulcerative colitis is a blend of Aloe vera, Calendula, and Chamomile. Aloe vera soothes and calms the digestive tract, reducing inflammation. Calendula promotes wound healing and tissue repair, while Chamomile alleviates stress and anxiety, common triggers of flare-ups. Additionally, incorporating Turmeric and Ginger into the cream can further reduce inflammation and promote overall gut health. This combination of herbs has been traditionally used to alleviate symptoms of ulcerative colitis and promote natural healing.
intro_study: For example, according to a study published by the Saudi journal of gastroenterology : official journal of the Saudi Gastroenterology Association, herbal creams derived from Calendula officinalis have shown potential in treating ulcerative colitis due to their anti-inflammatory properties. The study involved 96 rats with induced UC, where those treated with a 20% intracolonic gel form of Calendula officinalis extract and 3000 mg/kg of the extract orally showed significant improvement in body weight and reduction in oxidative stress indices, including malondialdehyde (MDA) level and myeloperoxidase (MPO) activity. These results indicate that Calendula officinalis extract can be a potential therapeutic agent for UC, suggesting that herbal creams containing this extract may be beneficial in managing the condition.
